clean_story:
Expectation reshapes reality. The brain heals itself.

The term placebo comes from Latin "I shall please"—because these inert treatments "please" the mind into triggering real physical changes.

A sugar pill doesn’t cure, yet the brain reacts as if it does. Here’s how: When you believe relief is coming, your prefrontal cortex (the brain’s "expectation manager") signals the spinal cord to release natural painkillers—endorphins and dopamine. It’s like a thermostat adjusting temperature before the heater even turns on. In one study, Parkinson’s patients given placebo injections showed a 200% spike in dopamine—same as real medication. Even open-label placebos (patients told they’re fake) reduce pain by 30%, proving belief alone rewires perception.

Surgeons once performed sham knee operations (incisions, no repairs). 60% of patients walked easier—scars healed faster than in no-treatment groups. The brain had "prepared" for recovery, boosting immune activity near the wound.

Three related subjects are pain’s neural pathways, nocebo effects reversed, and how rituals alter biology.
